title_en: Discourse on the Six Sections and Visceral Manifestations
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard that heaven forms a year through six periods of six, while man corresponds to this through the framework of nine-nine, and that man likewise has three hundred sixty-five joints, matching heaven and earth from ancient times — yet I do not understand what this means. Qibo replied: What an illuminating question! Let me explain it fully. The six-six periods and the nine-nine correspondence are the means by which the measures of heaven and the numbers of qi are made correct. The measures of heaven govern the movements of the sun and moon; the numbers of qi record the workings of transformation and generation.

Heaven is yang and earth is yin; the sun is yang and the moon is yin. Their movements have divisions and order, their cycles have paths and patterns. The sun travels one degree each day, the moon travels thirteen degrees and a fraction more, so that through the alternation of long and short months, three hundred sixty-five days make up a year, and the accumulated surplus of qi fills out into an intercalary month. One establishes the starting point at the beginning, corrects the markers at the midpoint, and calculates the remainder at the end, and thus the measures of heaven are complete.

Huangdi said: I have now heard of the measures of heaven; I wish to hear how the numbers of qi correspond to them. Qibo said: Heaven takes six-six as its period, earth takes nine-nine as its framework of correspondence. Heaven has ten stems; when the stems have cycled six times, a sexagenary cycle is complete; when the sexagenary cycle repeats six times, the year is complete — this is the rule of three hundred sixty days. From ancient times, that which connects with heaven is the root of life, and this root lies in yin and yang. The qi of the nine provinces and the nine orifices of the body all connect with the qi of heaven. Thus it generates five, and its qi is three; three completes heaven, three completes earth, three completes man; three multiplied by three makes nine; nine divides into nine fields, and the nine fields correspond to the nine viscera. Hence there are four visceral forms and five spirit viscera, together constituting the nine viscera that correspond to heaven and earth.

Huangdi said: I have now heard of the correspondence of six-six and nine-nine. You have spoken of accumulated qi filling out into intercalation; I wish to hear what is meant by qi. Please, Master, dispel my ignorance and resolve my doubts. Qibo said: This was kept secret by the high sovereigns of antiquity and handed down by former teachers. Huangdi said: Please let me hear it fully. Qibo said: Five days are called a watch; three watches are called a qi-period; six qi-periods are called a season; four seasons are called a year, and each governs its own rule. The five movements succeed one another and each in turn governs; when the cycle of the year is complete, it turns and begins again — the seasons are established and the qi is distributed like a ring without end, and the watches follow the same pattern. Hence it is said: one who does not know what is added to the year, the flourishing and decline of qi, and where deficiency and excess arise, cannot be considered a skilled physician.

Huangdi said: The beginnings of the five movements are like a ring without end — what of their excess and insufficiency? Qibo said: The five qi arise in succession, each having what it overcomes; the changes of flourishing and deficiency are their constant pattern. Huangdi said: What is a balanced qi? Qibo said: It is one without excess. Huangdi said: What of excess and insufficiency? Qibo said: These are recorded in the classics. Huangdi said: What is meant by 'what it overcomes'? Qibo said: Spring overcomes late summer, late summer overcomes winter, winter overcomes summer, summer overcomes autumn, autumn overcomes spring — this is called the mutual overcoming of the seasons according to the five phases, each naming its viscus by its qi. Huangdi said: How does one know which overcomes? Qibo said: One seeks its arrival, always returning to the start of spring. If it arrives before its time, this is called excess; it then oppresses what it cannot overcome and rides roughshod over what it overcomes — this is called excessive qi. One cannot then distinguish whether evil and deviance arise internally, and the physician cannot prevent it. If it has not arrived by its time, this is called insufficiency; then what it normally overcomes acts recklessly, and that which it generates suffers illness, while that which it cannot overcome oppresses it — this is called qi under duress. What is meant by 'seeking its arrival' is the time when the qi arrives. If one carefully watches for that time, the qi's arrival can be anticipated; but if one misses the time and fails to observe correctly, the five modes of treatment cannot be distinguished, and evil and deviance arise internally, which the physician cannot prevent. Huangdi said: Can it ever fail to follow this succession? Qibo said: The qi of the blue heaven cannot be without constancy; when the qi fails to follow its succession, this is called abnormal, and when it is abnormal, change occurs. Huangdi said: When it is abnormal and changes, what then? Qibo said: When change arrives, sickness occupies its place; if it is what it overcomes, the illness is mild; if it is what it cannot overcome, the illness is severe; and if compounded further by being struck again by evil qi, death results. Hence, if it is not the proper time, the illness is mild; if it is the proper time, the illness is severe.

Huangdi said: Good. I have heard that when qi combines it gives rise to form, and that through its transformations one establishes its proper name. Regarding the movements of heaven and earth and the transformations of yin and yang, in relation to the myriad things, which are few and which are many — may I hear of this? Qibo said: What a thorough question! Heaven is so vast it cannot be measured, earth is so great it cannot be gauged. Since Your Majesty's spirit-like inquiry reaches so far, let me set forth its general pattern. Plants produce the five colors, and the transformations of the five colors cannot all be seen; plants produce the five flavors, and the excellence of the five flavors cannot be exhausted. People's cravings and desires differ, each connecting with what suits it. Heaven feeds man with the five qi, earth feeds man with the five flavors. The five qi enter through the nose and are stored in the heart and lungs, causing the five colors above to become clear and lustrous and the voice to become resonant. The five flavors enter through the mouth and are stored in the intestines and stomach; the flavors are stored so as to nourish the five qi. When the qi is harmonious, fluid and humor are thereby generated, and the spirit then arises of itself.

Huangdi said: What of the manifestations of the viscera? Qibo said: The heart is the root of life, the seat of the transformations of spirit; its bloom is in the face, its fullness is in the blood vessels; it is the greater yang within yang, and it connects with the qi of summer. The lung is the root of qi, the dwelling place of the corporeal soul; its bloom is in the body hair, its fullness is in the skin; it is the lesser yin within yang, and it connects with the qi of autumn. The kidney governs hibernation and storage, and is the root of sealing and storing, the dwelling place of essence; its bloom is in the hair of the head, its fullness is in the bones; it is the lesser yin within yin, and it connects with the qi of winter. The liver is the root of endurance against exhaustion, the residence of the ethereal soul; its bloom is in the nails, its fullness is in the sinews; it generates blood and qi; its flavor is sour, its color is blue-green; it is the lesser yang within yang, and it connects with the qi of spring. The spleen, stomach, large intestine, small intestine, triple burner, and bladder are the root of the granary and storehouse, the residence of the nutritive qi; they are called vessels, able to transform waste matter, turning over flavors so that they pass in and out; their bloom is in the four whites around the lips, their fullness is in the flesh; their flavor is sweet, their color is yellow; these belong to the category of utmost yin, connecting with the qi of earth. In all, the function of the eleven viscera depends upon the gallbladder.

Therefore, when the Renying pulse at the neck is one degree stronger than normal, the disease is in the lesser yang; when two degrees stronger, the disease is in the greater yang; when three degrees stronger, the disease is in the yang brightness; when four degrees or more, this is called blocked yang. When the Cunkou pulse at the wrist is one degree stronger, the disease is in the reverting yin; when two degrees stronger, the disease is in the lesser yin; when three degrees stronger, the disease is in the greater yin; when four degrees or more, this is called barred yin. When both the Renying and Cunkou pulses are four times stronger than normal, this is called barred-and-blocked; when the pulse of barred-and-blocked becomes excessive, it can no longer reach the utmost essential qi of heaven and earth, and death follows.

title_en: On the Generation and Formation of the Five Viscera
paragraphs_en: The heart pairs with the blood vessels; its bloom shows in the complexion; it is governed by the kidney. The lung pairs with the skin; its bloom shows in the body hair; it is governed by the heart. The liver pairs with the sinews; its bloom shows in the nails; it is governed by the lung. The spleen pairs with the flesh; its bloom shows in the lips; it is governed by the liver. The kidney pairs with the bones; its bloom shows in the hair of the head; it is governed by the spleen.

Therefore, eating too much salty food causes the blood vessels to congeal and stagnate, changing the complexion; eating too much bitter food causes the skin to wither and the body hair to fall out; eating too much pungent food causes the sinews to tighten and the nails to wither; eating too much sour food causes the flesh to become calloused and thick and the lips to turn outward and crack; eating too much sweet food causes the bones to ache and the hair to fall out — these are the injuries caused by the five flavors. Thus the heart desires bitter, the lung desires pungent, the liver desires sour, the spleen desires sweet, and the kidney desires salty — these are the correspondences of the five flavors to the five viscera. As for the qi of the five viscera: if the complexion appears as green as dead grass, death follows; as yellow as the fruit of the trifoliate orange, death follows; as black as soot, death follows; as red as clotted blood, death follows; as white as dried bone, death follows — these are the five colors that indicate death. If the complexion is as green as a kingfisher's feathers, life is indicated; as red as a cockscomb, life is indicated; as yellow as a crab's belly, life is indicated; as white as pig fat, life is indicated; as black as a crow's feathers, life is indicated — these are the five colors that indicate life. A healthy complexion generated from the heart is like vermilion wrapped in white silk; generated from the lung is like crimson wrapped in white silk; generated from the liver is like dark blue-green wrapped in white silk; generated from the spleen is like the fruit of the trichosanthes wrapped in white silk; generated from the kidney is like purple wrapped in white silk — these are the outward blooms produced by the five viscera. Colors and flavors correspond to the five viscera: white corresponds to the lung and to pungent flavor; red corresponds to the heart and to bitter flavor; green corresponds to the liver and to sour flavor; yellow corresponds to the spleen and to sweet flavor; black corresponds to the kidney and to salty flavor. Likewise, white corresponds to the skin, red to the blood vessels, green to the sinews, yellow to the flesh, and black to the bones.

All the blood vessels belong to the eyes; all the marrow belongs to the brain; all the sinews belong to the joints; all the blood belongs to the heart; all the qi belongs to the lung — these govern the rising and settling of qi and blood through the four limbs and the eight great junctures. Thus when a person lies down, the blood returns to the liver; when the liver receives blood, the eyes are able to see; when the feet receive blood, they are able to walk; when the palms receive blood, they are able to grasp; when the fingers receive blood, they are able to hold. If, upon rising from lying down, one is struck by wind, blood that congeals in the skin causes numbness; blood that congeals in the vessels causes stagnation; blood that congeals in the feet causes cold reversal. In these three cases, the blood moves but cannot return to its proper cavity, and thus numbness and cold reversal occur. The human body has twelve great valleys and three hundred fifty-four small ravines, minus twelve transport points — these are all places where defensive qi lodges and where evil qi may take up residence; needles and stone lancets should be applied along these to remove it.

In diagnosing the onset of disease, the five determinations serve as the guiding principle. To know the origin of a disease, one must first establish its root — what is called the five determinations are the five pulses. Thus, in headache and disorders of the vertex, with deficiency below and excess above, the fault lies in the foot lesser yin; if severe, it enters the greater yang. In dizziness, spinning, staggering, blurred vision, and deafness, with excess below and deficiency above, the fault lies in the foot lesser yang; if severe, it enters the reverting yin. In abdominal fullness and distension, with support failing at the diaphragm and flanks, with reversal below and clouding above, the fault lies in the foot greater yin and yang brightness. In coughing and rising qi, with reversal in the chest, the fault lies in the hand yang brightness and greater yin. In vexation of the heart and headache, with the disease located within the diaphragm, the fault lies in the hand greater yang and lesser yin.

As for the pulse's smallness or largeness, smoothness or roughness, floating or sinking quality — these can be distinguished by the fingers; the manifestations of the five viscera can be inferred by category; the correspondence of the five viscera to the five tones can be understood by intuition; and subtle diagnosis by the five colors can be observed with the eyes. One who can combine examination of the pulse and the complexion can achieve complete accuracy. When the red pulse arrives panting and hard, the diagnosis is: there is accumulated qi within, which at times interferes with eating — this is called heart-numbness, acquired from external illness, from worry and overthinking that empties the heart, so that evil qi follows and enters. When the white pulse arrives panting and floating, with deficiency above and excess below, there is fright and accumulated qi in the chest, panting with deficiency — this is called lung-numbness with cold and heat, acquired from intoxication followed by sexual intercourse. When the blue-green pulse arrives long, twanging left and right like a plucked string, there is accumulated qi below the heart supporting the flanks — this is called liver-numbness, acquired from cold and dampness, following the same pattern as hernia, with lower back pain, cold feet, and headache. When the yellow pulse arrives large and empty, there is accumulated qi in the abdomen and reversal qi called reversal-hernia — women follow the same pattern — acquired from exerting the four limbs to the point of sweating and then being struck by wind. When the black pulse arrives hard above and large, there is accumulated qi in the lower abdomen and genital region — this is called kidney-numbness, acquired from bathing in cold water and then lying down.

In general, when observing the five colors together with an unusual pulse: a yellow face with green eyes, a yellow face with red eyes, a yellow face with white eyes, or a yellow face with black eyes — in all these cases the patient will not die. A green face with red eyes, a red face with white eyes, a green face with black eyes, a black face with white eyes, or a red face with green eyes — in all these cases the patient will die.

title_en: Further Discourse on the Five Viscera
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard that some practitioners of the arts consider the brain and marrow to be viscera, while others consider the intestines and stomach to be viscera, and still others consider them to be bowels. Daring to ask — these views contradict one another, yet each claims to be correct, and I do not understand the reasoning behind them. I wish to hear your explanation. Qibo replied: The brain, the marrow, the bones, the blood vessels, the gallbladder, and the uterus — these six are generated by the qi of earth; they all store yin and take earth as their model, and so they store without discharging; they are called the extraordinary bowels. As for the stomach, large intestine, small intestine, triple burner, and bladder — these five are generated by the qi of heaven; their qi resembles heaven, and so they discharge without storing. These receive the turbid qi of the five viscera and are called the bowels of conveyance and transformation; they cannot retain their contents for long, but convey and discharge them. The anus also serves the five viscera, ensuring that water and grain are not retained for long. What are called the five viscera store essential qi without discharging it, and so they may be full but cannot be replete. The six bowels convey and transform substances without storing them, and so they may be replete but cannot be full. The reason for this is: when water and grain enter the mouth, the stomach becomes replete while the intestines remain empty; when the food descends, the intestines become replete while the stomach becomes empty. Hence it is said: replete but not full, full but not replete.

Huangdi said: Why is the qi opening at the wrist alone able to serve as the governor of the five viscera? Qibo said: The stomach is the sea of water and grain, the great source of the six bowels. The five flavors enter the mouth and are stored in the stomach, which nourishes the qi of the five viscera. The qi opening is also connected to the greater yin. Thus the qi and flavor of the five viscera and six bowels all issue from the stomach and manifest as changes at the qi opening. Therefore, the five qi enter through the nose and are stored in the heart and lung; when the heart and lung are diseased, the nose in turn becomes obstructed.

In treating disease, one must always examine the patient's lower condition, adjust to the pulse, and observe the patient's intentions and state of mind, as well as their illness. With those bound by superstition of ghosts and spirits, one cannot speak of the utmost virtue of medicine; with those who abhor needles and stone lancets, one cannot speak of the utmost skill. If a patient refuses treatment, the disease certainly cannot be cured, and treating it will be without effect.

title_en: Discourse on Different Methods Suited to Different Regions
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: When physicians treat disease, for one and the same disease the treatments differ, yet all achieve a cure — why is this? Qibo replied: This is caused by the differing configurations of the land. Thus, in the eastern region, the place where heaven and earth first give birth to life, a land of fish and salt bordering the sea, its people eat fish and crave salty food; they are content with their dwelling places and relish their food. Fish causes heat to accumulate within a person, and salt overcomes the blood; thus the people there all have dark complexions and coarse skin texture, and their diseases are mostly abscesses and ulcers. Their proper treatment is stone lancets; hence stone lancets also originated from the east.

The western region is a land of metal and jade, of sand and stone, the place where heaven and earth gather in and draw together. Its people dwell on the highlands, and there is much wind; the water and soil are hard and strong. The people there do not wear cloth but coarse felt and mats; they eat rich food and are fat. Thus evil qi cannot injure their bodily form, and their diseases arise from within; the proper treatment is toxic medicines. Hence toxic medicines also originated from the west.

The northern region is the land where heaven and earth close up and store away. The land there is elevated, and the people dwell on the highlands, with wind, cold, and ice. The people there delight in living in the open wilds and subsist on milk; cold accumulates in the viscera, giving rise to diseases of fullness and distension. The proper treatment is moxibustion with burning artemisia; hence moxibustion also originated from the north.

The southern region is where heaven and earth foster growth, the place where yang is most abundant. Its land lies low, and the water and soil are weak; it is a place where mist and dew gather. Its people crave sour food and eat fermented foods; thus the people there all have fine skin texture and reddish complexions, and their diseases are cramp and numbness. The proper treatment is fine needles; hence the nine needles also originated from the south.

The central region has land that is flat and moist, and it is where heaven and earth produce the greatest abundance of the myriad things. Its people eat a mixed diet and do not labor much; thus their diseases are mostly wilting, cold reversal, cold, and heat. The proper treatment is guided stretching and massage; hence guided stretching and massage also originated from the center. Therefore the sage combines and integrates these methods in treatment, each suited to what is appropriate, so that although the treatments differ, the diseases are all cured — this comes from grasping the true nature of the disease and understanding the overall principles of treatment.

title_en: Discourse on Shifting the Essence and Transforming the Qi
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard that in treating disease in ancient times, one could cure illness merely by shifting the essence and transforming the qi, through invocation and incantation alone. In treating disease today, toxic medicines treat the interior and needles and stone lancets treat the exterior, yet sometimes the patient is cured and sometimes not — why is this? Qibo replied: In ancient times, people dwelled among the birds and beasts; they exerted themselves in movement to avoid the cold, and dwelled in the shade to avoid the heat. Within, they had no entanglements of longing and attachment; without, they had no ambition to pursue office. In this age of tranquil indifference, evil qi could not penetrate deeply. Thus toxic medicines were not needed to treat the interior, nor needles and stone to treat the exterior, and so it was sufficient to shift the essence and use incantation alone. The present age is not so: worries and troubles beset people within, toil and hardship injure their bodies without; moreover, they have lost accord with the four seasons and go against the proper measures of cold and heat. Injurious winds arrive frequently, and depleting evils come morning and night, penetrating within to the five viscera and marrow and bones, and injuring without the orifices, muscles, and skin. Hence minor illnesses inevitably become severe, and severe illnesses inevitably lead to death; therefore incantation alone can no longer cure.

Huangdi said: Good. I wish, when attending a patient, to be able to observe whether they will live or die and resolve uncertain cases; I want to know the essentials of this, as clear as sun and moon — may I hear of it? Qibo said: Complexion and pulse are what the high sovereigns valued and what former teachers transmitted. In high antiquity, the physician Jiudai Ji ordered complexion and pulse and thereby penetrated to spirit-like clarity, uniting them with metal, wood, water, fire, and earth, the four seasons, the eight winds, and the six confluences, never departing from their constancy; their transformations shift into one another, and by observing their subtlety one comes to know the essentials. If one wishes to know the essentials, then complexion and pulse are precisely this. Complexion corresponds to the sun, and the pulse corresponds to the moon; if one constantly seeks their essentials, this indeed is the essential. As for the changes and transformations of complexion, corresponding to the pulse of the four seasons — this is what the high sovereigns valued, uniting with spirit-like clarity, and by this one keeps death at a distance and draws near to life. When the way of life is thereby prolonged, this is called a sage-king. In the treatment of disease in middle antiquity, one treated as soon as illness arrived: decoctions were used for ten days to dispel diseases of the eight winds and the five kinds of numbness; if not resolved in ten days, one treated with the branches and stalks of herbs, the root and tip aiding each other; once root and branch of treatment were both attained, the evil qi would submit. The treatment of disease in recent generations is not so: treatment does not take the four seasons as its basis, one does not know the movements of sun and moon, and one does not examine whether the condition is contrary or compliant. Only after the form of the disease has already taken shape does one then wish to use fine needles to treat the exterior and decoctions to treat the interior. Crude practitioners, acting rashly and violently, believe they can attack the disease directly; thus before the original illness is resolved, a new illness arises in its place.

Huangdi said: I wish to hear the essential Way. Qibo said: The utmost essential of treatment lies in never losing sight of complexion and pulse; when one applies this without confusion, this is the great principle of treatment. If one acts contrary to compliance, and root and branch are not properly grasped, the spirit is lost and the state of the body is ruined. Discard the old and embrace the new — only then does one attain the understanding of the Realized Person. Huangdi said: I have now heard the essentials from you, Master; you have said it lies in not departing from complexion and pulse — this I now understand. Qibo said: The utmost of treatment lies in unity. Huangdi said: What is meant by unity? Qibo said: Unity is attained through grasping the root cause. Huangdi said: How is this done? Qibo said: Close the doors, shut the windows, attend closely to the patient, repeatedly inquire into their condition so as to follow their state of mind. One who grasps the patient's spirit will flourish; one who loses the patient's spirit will perish. Huangdi said: Good.

title_en: Discourse on Decoctions and Sweet Wine Ferments
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: How does one make decoctions and sweet wine ferments from the five grains? Qibo replied: One must use rice, and cook it with rice stalks as fuel; rice grain is complete in its qi, and rice stalks are firm. Huangdi said: Why is this so? Qibo said: Rice obtains the harmony of heaven and earth and the suitability of high and low ground, and so it can reach completeness; it is harvested and cut at the proper time, and so it can reach firmness. Huangdi said: Why did the sages of high antiquity make decoctions and sweet wine ferments yet not use them? Qibo said: Since ancient times, the sages who made decoctions and sweet wine ferments did so only to have them in readiness; in high antiquity, decoctions were made but not taken. In middle antiquity, when virtue had somewhat declined, evil qi arrived from time to time, and taking them ensured complete recovery. Huangdi said: Why is it that in the present age this is not always sufficient? Qibo said: In the present age, one must use toxic medicines to attack disease within, and lancet stones, needles, and moxa to treat disease without.

Huangdi said: Why is it that sometimes the bodily form is exhausted and the blood depleted, yet no effect is achieved? Qibo said: The spirit is not being made use of. Huangdi said: What is meant by 'the spirit is not being made use of'? Qibo said: Needles and stone lancets follow the Way only when the patient's spirit cooperates. If essence and spirit do not advance, and the will and intent are not well ordered, then the disease cannot be cured. Now, when essence is ruined and spirit has departed, the nutritive and defensive qi can no longer be recovered. Why is this? Because cravings and desires are endless, and worries and troubles never cease; essence and qi become slack and ruined, the nutritive qi stagnates and the defensive qi is dispersed. Hence the spirit departs and the disease is not cured.

Huangdi said: Now, when a disease first arises, it is extremely subtle and extremely fine, and must first enter and bind in the skin. Yet skilled physicians all say: once a disease is fully formed, it is called contrary, and then needles and stone cannot treat it, nor can good medicine reach it. Now, even when skilled physicians have all grasped the method and adhere to its principles, with relatives near and far constantly heard in the patient's ear and the five colors constantly seen by the patient's eyes, and yet the disease is still not cured — how could there be any leisure left for delay? Qibo said: The disease is the root, the physician is the branch; when root and branch do not correspond, the evil qi will not submit — this is what is meant.

Huangdi said: There are cases where illness does not arise from the fine hairs of the exterior, but where the yang of the five viscera is exhausted, fluid fills the outer frame of the body, the corporeal soul dwells alone, essence is isolated within, and qi is drained without, so that the body can no longer be kept warm even with clothing. In such cases the four extremities become tense and affect the center — this is a state in which qi is repelled within while the swollen form is displayed without. How should this be treated? Qibo said: Regulate and balance it as with a steelyard, remove what is stagnant and stale, gently move the four extremities, keep the patient warmly clothed, and apply contralateral needling to the affected areas, so as to restore the bodily form. Open the ghost gate, cleanse the clean palace, let the essence be replenished in its proper time, so that the five yang qi are spread throughout, the five viscera are cleared and drained, and thus essence will regenerate of itself, the bodily form will flourish of itself, bone and flesh will support each other, and the great qi will then become balanced. Huangdi said: Good.

title_en: Discourse on the Essentials Inscribed on the Jade Tablet
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard of assessing measures and of the extraordinary and the constant, and that what they refer to differs — how are they to be applied? Qibo replied: Assessing measures means gauging the depth of a disease. The extraordinary refers to unusual diseases. Let me speak of the ultimate reckoning of the Way: the five colors, the changes of the pulse, assessing measures, and the extraordinary and the constant — the Way lies in unity. The spirit turns and does not reverse; if it reverses, it no longer turns, and thereby loses its pivot. The essentials of the ultimate reckoning are pressing and close, yet subtle; when inscribed on jade tablets, this is called uniting with the jade pivot.

When facial complexion appears in the upper, lower, left, and right regions of the face, each has its own essential meaning. If the color appears shallow, decoctions govern the treatment, and the disease will resolve within ten days. If it appears deep, potent formulas of medicine must govern the treatment, and it will resolve within twenty-one days. If it appears extremely deep, sweet wine ferments govern the treatment, and it will resolve within a hundred days. If the complexion is withered and the face gaunt, it cannot be treated, and the patient will die within a hundred days. If the pulse is short and the qi cut off, death follows; in warm disease with extreme deficiency, death follows. When complexion appears in the upper, lower, left, and right, each has its own essential meaning: appearing above indicates a contrary condition, appearing below indicates a compliant condition. In women, complexion appearing on the right is contrary and the left is compliant; in men, the left is contrary and the right is compliant. A change resulting in doubled yang leads to death, and doubled yin leads to death.

When yin and yang reverse into their opposites, treatment lies in weighing and balancing to seize back the advantage — these are matters of the extraordinary and matters of assessing measures. A pounding, struggling pulse indicates numbness and paralysis, the interaction of cold and heat. An isolated pulse indicates dissipation of qi; deficiency with diarrhea indicates loss of blood. An isolated pulse is contrary; a deficient pulse is compliant. In applying the method of the extraordinary and the constant, begin with the greater yin. To proceed toward what one cannot overcome is called contrary, and being contrary leads to death; to proceed toward what one can overcome is called compliant, and being compliant leads to survival. The overcoming relationships of the eight winds and four seasons end and begin again in cycle; if one deviates from this succession even once, it can no longer be reckoned. Thus the essentials of this discourse are complete.
